source_text_a: Little Gidding is the fourth and final poem of T. S. Eliot 's Four Quartets , a series of poems that discuss time , perspective , humanity , and salvation.
source_text_b: Little Gidding is the first poem of T. S. Eliot 's Four Quartets.
explanation: Sentence 2 says Little Gidding is the fourth and final poem of Four Quartets, while sentence 46 says it is the first poem, which cannot both be true.

source_text_a: The title refers to a small Anglican community in Huntingdonshire , established by Nicholas Ferrar in the 17th century and scattered during the English Civil War.
source_text_b: Little Gidding was the home of an Anglican community established in 1626 by Nicholas Ferrar.
explanation: Sentence 4 says the community was established in the 17th century, while sentence 19 specifies it was established in 1626; these dates conflict in the document's description of the founding.

source_text_a: The title refers to a small Anglican community in Huntingdonshire , established by Nicholas Ferrar in the 17th century and scattered during the English Civil War.
source_text_b: The original Little Gidding community was built for living on monastic lines , but the community was damaged and dispersed by Puritan forces during the English Civil War in 1646.
explanation: Sentence 4 says the community was scattered during the English Civil War, while sentence 40 says it was dispersed in 1646 by Puritan forces; these give conflicting timing/details for the same event.

source_text_a: The poem was finished by 19 September 1942 and published in the October New English Weekly.
source_text_b: It was first published in September 1942 after being delayed for over a year because of the air - raids on Great Britain during World War II and Eliot 's declining health.
explanation: Sentence 17 says the poem was published in the October New English Weekly, while sentence 3 says it was first published in September 1942, so the publication timing does not match.

source_text_a: The outlines of a nuclear deal with Iran are in place.
source_text_b: The framework we have before us keeps Iran's nuclear door well and truly open.
explanation: Sentence 1 says the outlines of a nuclear deal are already in place, while sentence 30 says the framework keeps Iran's nuclear door open, which is a conflicting characterization of the deal's status/effect in the same argument.

source_text_a: President Obama once had a tougher line, when in 2012 he said: "The deal we'll accept is they end their nuclear program.
source_text_b: Negotiated between Iran and the United States, Russia, China, Britain, France and Germany, the framework concedes that Iran can maintain "a mutually defined enrichment program," operate thousands of centrifuges, and continue its research and development of nuclear technologies.
explanation: Sentence 9 claims Obama said the deal they would accept is one in which Iran ends its nuclear program, but sentence 12 says the framework explicitly allows Iran to maintain enrichment, centrifuges, and nuclear R&D.

source_text_a: President Obama once had a tougher line, when in 2012 he said: "The deal we'll accept is they end their nuclear program.
source_text_b: The framework we have before us keeps Iran's nuclear door well and truly open.
explanation: Sentence 9 describes an acceptable deal as one that ends Iran's nuclear program, whereas sentence 30 says the framework keeps Iran's nuclear door open.

source_text_a: The individual members of the fraternity will likely be considered private individuals, and not public figures; the latter have a harder time proving defamation.
source_text_b: The individual members of the fraternity will likely be considered public figures, and not private individuals;
explanation: Sentence 14 says the individual fraternity members will likely be private individuals, while sentence 38 says the same members will likely be public figures; these are mutually exclusive statuses in the same context.

source_text_a: A private person suing for defamation must establish that the defendant has published a false factual statement that is about the person and that it also harms the person's reputation.
source_text_b: The individual members of the fraternity will likely be considered public figures, and not private individuals;
explanation: Sentence 15 frames the plaintiff as a private person needing the private-figure defamation standard, but sentence 38 says the fraternity members are public figures, which changes the applicable plaintiff category and conflicts with sentence 15's description.

source_text_a: He had a guest role in the television series Judge John Deed in 2002.In 2004 Boulter landed a role as " Craig " in the episode " Teddy 's Story " of the television series The Long Firm ; he did not star alongside actors Mark Strong and Derek Jacobi.
source_text_b: In 2004 Boulter landed a role as " Craig " in the episode " Teddy 's Story " of the television series The Long Firm ; he starred alongside actors Mark Strong and Derek Jacobi.
explanation: Sentence 5 says Boulter did not star alongside Mark Strong and Derek Jacobi in The Long Firm, while sentence 22 says he did star alongside those actors in the same role context.

source_text_a: Boulter starred as " Scott " in the play Herons written by Simon Stephens , which was performed in 2001 at the Royal Court Theatre.
source_text_b: Boulter starred as " Darren " , in the 2005 theatre productions of the Philip Ridley play Mercury Fur.
explanation: Sentence 18 says he starred as "Scott" in Herons, while sentence 23 says he starred as "Darren" in Herons, giving incompatible character identities for the same performance.

source_text_a: He appeared in the television series Judge John Deed in 2002 as " Addem Armitage " in the episode " Political Expediency " , and had a role as a different character " Toby Steele " on The Bill.
source_text_b: He had a recurring role in 2003 on two episodes of The Bill , as character " Connor Price ".
explanation: Sentence 20 says Boulter had a role on The Bill in 2002, while sentence 21 places his recurring role on The Bill in 2003, which conflicts on the same series appearance timeline in this document context.

source_text_a: In 2006 Boulter starred in the play Citizenship written by Mark Ravenhill.
source_text_b: In 2006 , Boulter starred alongside Whishaw in the play Citizenship written by Mark Ravenhill.
explanation: Sentence 29 says he starred in Citizenship in 2006, while sentence 8 says he starred alongside Ben Whishaw in Citizenship in 2006; these are compatible, so this is not a contradiction.

source_text_a: In one of Villette's famous plot twists, "Dr. John" is later revealed to be John Graham Bretton, a fact that Lucy has known but has deliberately concealed from the reader.
source_text_b: After Dr. John (i.e., Graham) discovers Ginevra's unworthiness, he turns his attention to Lucy, and they become close friends.
explanation: Sentence 19 says Lucy has known all along that Dr. John is John Graham Bretton, while sentence 20 describes Graham later discovering his identity and then shifting his attention to Lucy, which conflicts with the claim that Lucy already knew and concealed this fact.

source_text_a: The LHC generates up to 600 million particles per second, with a beam circulating for 10 hours, traveling more than 6 billion miles (more than 10 billion kilometers) -- the distance from Earth to Neptune and back again.
source_text_b: The LHC generates only 100 particles per second.
explanation: Sentence 6 says the LHC generates up to 600 million particles per second, while sentence 8 says it generates only 100 particles per second.

source_text_a: Capel Lligwy ( sometimes referred to as Hen Gapel Lligwy ) is a ruined chapel near Rhos Lligwy in Anglesey , north Wales , dating back to the first half of the 12th century.
source_text_b: Capel Lligwy is a well-preserved chapel near Rhos Lligwy in Anglesey, north Wales, dating back to the first half of the 12th century.
explanation: Sentence 2 says the chapel is ruined, while sentence 11 describes it as well-preserved, which are incompatible condition descriptions.

source_text_a: It was used for a time until the early 18th century as a private place of worship for a nearby house , then later fell into disrepair.
source_text_b: After the early part of the 18th century , however , the chapel became unused and began to fall into disrepair.
explanation: Sentence 4 says it was used until the early 18th century and then fell into disrepair, while sentence 22 says that after the early 18th century it became unused and began to fall into disrepair; these are compatible and not contradictory.

source_text_a: The current record-holder for top April opening domestically is "Captain America: The Winter Soldier," which debuted to $95 million from 3,928 theaters last year."Fast & Furious 6," debuted to a franchise-worst $30 million over the four-day Memorial Day weekend in 2012 "Furious 7" is likewise poised to nab the biggest opening of 2015 to date.
source_text_b: The last film, "Fast & Furious 6," debuted to a franchise-best $117 million over the four-day Memorial Day weekend in 2012, including $97.4 million for the three days, on its way to grossing $788.7 million worldwide.
explanation: Sentence 7 says 'Fast & Furious 6' debuted to a franchise-worst $30 million over the four-day Memorial Day weekend, while sentence 10 says it debuted to a franchise-best $117 million over the same four-day weekend.

source_text_a: The French cruiser Sully was an armored cruiser of the Gloire class that was built for the French Navy in the early 1900s.
source_text_b: The French cruiser Sully was an armored cruiser of the Gueydon class.
explanation: Sentence 2 says Sully was a Gloire-class armored cruiser, while sentence 27 says she was a Gueydon-class armored cruiser.

source_text_a: The ship struck a rock in Hạ Long Bay , French Indochina in 1905 , only eight months after she was completed , and was a total loss.
source_text_b: On 7 February 1905 Sully struck a rock in Hạ Long Bay ; her crew was not injured.
explanation: Sentence 4 says the ship struck the rock in 1905 only eight months after completion, while sentence 28 gives the specific date 7 February 1905, which is consistent in context but conflicts with the less specific timing only if read as a different date; however the key mismatch is not strong enough for a contradiction, so this pair is borderline.

source_text_a: Josepha Petrick Kemarre ( born ca. 1945 or ca. 1953 , date uncertain ) is an Anmatyerre - speaking Indigenous Australian from Central Australia.
source_text_b: Josepha Petrick Kemarre is an Anmatyerre - speaking Indigenous Australian , born around 1945 or 1953 at the Santa Teresa Mission , near Alice Springs in Australia 's Northern Territory.
explanation: Josepha Petrick Kemarre is given two incompatible birth years: circa 1945 or circa 1953 in sentence 2, and likewise around 1945 or 1953 in sentence 10, but the issue is that the document presents mutually inconsistent uncertainty ranges without resolving a single consistent birth year; however this is not a true contradiction.},{

source_text_a: What became NY 31B was not originally designated as NY 293 as part of the 1930 renumbering of state highways in New York.
source_text_b: What became NY 31B was originally designated as NY 293 as part of the 1930 renumbering of state highways in New York.
explanation: Sentence 3 says the route was not originally designated NY 293, while sentence 16 says it was originally designated NY 293.

source_text_a: NY 31B was assigned c. 1933 , replacing New York State Route 293 , a route assigned as part of the 1930 renumbering of state highways in New York.
source_text_b: NY 293 was renumbered to NY 31B c. 1933 , allowing the NY 293 designation to be transferred to another highway in Orange County.
explanation: Sentence 5 says NY 31B replaced NY 293, but sentence 17 says NY 293 was renumbered to NY 31B, which is the opposite relationship for the route history.

source_text_a: The 2010 Claxton Shield was contested between five teams from around Australia.
source_text_b: Each team held their home games at multiple venues.
explanation: Sentence 28 says the tournament was contested between five teams, while sentence 29 says each team held home games at multiple venues, which cannot coexist with sentence 31's later claim that each team held home games at only one venue; however 28 and 29 themselves do not directly conflict, so no contradiction pair here.

source_text_a: Sylvia leaves her father's house to mourn her brother Owen's death.
source_text_b: She tells her father Balance that she is going to the Welsh countryside but in fact goes into Shrewsbury dressed as a man, under the name 'Jack Wilful'.
explanation: Sentence 9 says Sylvia leaves to mourn her brother's death, while sentence 10 says she instead goes into Shrewsbury in disguise, which are incompatible accounts of her immediate destination/action.

source_text_a: She tells her father Balance that she is going to the Welsh countryside but in fact goes into Shrewsbury dressed as a man, under the name 'Jack Wilful'.
source_text_b: Sylvia goes into Shrewsbury dressed as a woman.
explanation: Sentence 10 says Sylvia goes into Shrewsbury dressed as a man, while sentence 12 says she goes into Shrewsbury dressed as a woman, which conflicts on her disguise.

source_text_a: Still disguised as Wilful, Sylvia goes on to spend the night in bed with Rose, a local wench previously courted by Plume to get Rose's brother Bullock to join up.
source_text_b: An action is brought against 'Wilful' for sexually assaulting Rose and 'he' finds 'himself' on trial before Sylvia's father Balance and his two fellow magistrates Scruple and Scale.
explanation: Sentence 14 says Sylvia spends the night in bed with Rose while disguised as Wilful, but sentence 15 says Wilful is accused of sexually assaulting Rose, so the same encounter is described in incompatible terms.

source_text_a: The rhyme dates back at least to the 16th century.
source_text_b: The rhyme dates back at least to the 18th century and exists with different numbers of verses each with a number of variations.
explanation: Sentence 3 says the rhyme dates back at least to the 16th century, while sentence 5 says it dates back at least to the 18th century.

source_text_a: The phrase " Jack and Jill " , indicating a boy and a girl , was in use in England as early as the 16th century.
source_text_b: However , the woodcut that accompanied the first recorded version of the rhyme showed two boys ( not a boy and a girl ) , and used the spelling Gill not Jill.
explanation: Sentence 22 says the phrase indicated a boy and a girl, but sentence 25 says the earliest printed version showed two boys, not a boy and a girl.

source_text_a: North of Rochelle, a tornado took away a local favorite restaurant -- Grubsteakers.People had been eating at Grubsteakers for some 10 years
source_text_b: People had been eating at Grubsteakers for some 25 years and will miss it, Widick said.
explanation: The restaurant's history is given as being in business for some 10 years in one sentence but some 25 years in the other.

source_text_a: VanVickle was newly elected sheriff of Rochelle on Wednesday.
source_text_b: It was the first tornado the sheriff had ever seen in his county.
explanation: Sentence 37 says VanVickle was newly elected sheriff on Wednesday, while sentence 42 says this was the first tornado he had ever seen in his county; these can both be true, but the wording about his role and the timing is potentially inconsistent in context, though not a direct contradiction.

source_text_a: Both Sébergué and Ndikert ranked first in their respective heats and advanced past the qualification round.
source_text_b: Both Sébergué and Ndikert ranked seventh in their respective heats and did not advance past the qualification round.
explanation: Sentence 3 says both athletes ranked first in their heats and advanced, while sentence 5 says both ranked seventh and did not advance.

source_text_a: As of the end of the 2012 London Olympics , there have been no medalists from Chad.
source_text_b: Up to and including the Beijing games , there has yet to have been a medalist from Chad.
explanation: Sentence 6 says that as of the end of the 2012 Olympics there were no medalists from Chad, and sentence 20 says there has yet to have been a medalist from Chad up to and including Beijing, which is incompatible only if interpreted differently in time; however both are consistent, so no contradiction.

source_text_a: The United States has the dubious distinction of being the only country in the Americas to conduct executions, but the number of convicts put to death here fell slightly, from 39 in 2013 to 35 in 2014.
source_text_b: The United States is not the only country in the Americas to conduct executions
explanation: Sentence 23 says the United States is the only country in the Americas to conduct executions, while sentence 25 says the United States is not the only country in the Americas to conduct executions.

source_text_a: Dr Watson is called to tend Holmes, who is apparently dying of a rare disease contracted while he was on a case.
source_text_b: Holmes was never really dying.
explanation: Sentence 1 says Holmes is apparently dying of a rare disease, while sentence 26 says he was never really dying.

source_text_a: Dr Watson is called to tend Holmes, who is apparently dying of a rare disease contracted while he was on a case.
source_text_b: His feigned illness was a ruse to induce Smith to confess to his nephew’s murder.
explanation: Sentence 1 presents the illness as real, but sentence 27 states it was a feigned illness used as a ruse.

source_text_a: Dr Watson is called to tend Holmes, who is apparently dying of a rare disease contracted while he was on a case.
source_text_b: Eating normally for three days, and the claim of the "disease's" infectious nature was to keep Watson from examining him and discovering the ruse.
explanation: Sentence 1 describes Holmes as dying of disease, whereas sentence 29 says the disease claim was only a cover to prevent Watson from investigating.

source_text_a: Mrs. Hudson says that Holmes has neither eaten nor drunk anything in three days.
source_text_b: Eating normally for three days, and the claim of the "disease's" infectious nature was to keep Watson from examining him and discovering the ruse.
explanation: Sentence 3 says Holmes had eaten nor drunk anything in three days, but sentence 29 says he was eating normally for three days.

source_text_a: Holmes instructs Watson not to come near him, because the illness is highly infectious.
source_text_b: Eating normally for three days, and the claim of the "disease's" infectious nature was to keep Watson from examining him and discovering the ruse.
explanation: Sentence 4 says Holmes was highly infectious, while sentence 29 says that infectious claim was false and only meant to mislead Watson.

source_text_a: It soon emerges, to the hiding Watson’s horror, that Holmes has been sickened by the same illness that killed Smith’s nephew Victor.
source_text_b: Holmes was not infected by the little box; he has enough enemies to know that he must always examine his mail carefully before he opens it.
explanation: Sentence 17 says Holmes has been sickened by the same illness that killed Smith's nephew, but sentence 28 says Holmes was not infected by the little box.

source_text_a: It turns out a corporation can indeed be prosecuted like a person.
source_text_b: It raises a larger question, though: Why do we prosecute inanimate objects that we can't even incarcerate?
explanation: Sentence 10 says corporations can be prosecuted like persons, while sentence 50 says the text questions why we prosecute inanimate objects at all, which conflicts with the asserted corporate-prosecution framing.

source_text_a: The company maintains that it is innocent.
source_text_b: The indictment alleges that as early as 2004, FedEx knew that it was delivering drugs to dealers and addicts.
explanation: Sentence 17 says FedEx maintains it is innocent, but sentence 47 says the indictment alleges FedEx knew as early as 2004 that it was delivering drugs to dealers and addicts.

source_text_a: The Fastra II 's predecessor , the Fastra I , has 4 dual - GPU GeForce 9800 GX2 video cards , for a total of 8 GPUs.
source_text_b: The Fastra II has four faster dual - GPU GeForce GTX 295 video cards.
explanation: Sentence 9 says the Fastra I has 4 dual-GPU video cards, while sentence 29 says the Fastra II has 4 dual-GPU video cards; these statements are about different machines and are not contradictory.

source_text_a: The Fastra II has six faster dual - GPU GeForce GTX 295 video cards , and a single - GPU GeForce GTX 275 , for a total of 13 GPUs.
source_text_b: The total amount of GPUs is 13.
explanation: Sentence 13 explicitly says the total number of GPUs is 13, and sentence 30 repeats the same total, so they do not contradict.

source_text_a: WASP - 44 has an effective temperature of 6000 K , hotter than the Sun.
source_text_b: WASP - 44 has an effective temperature of 5410 K , cooler than the Sun.
explanation: Sentence 18 says WASP-44 is hotter than the Sun, while sentence 23 says it is cooler than the Sun, which cannot both be true for the same star in this context.

source_text_a: The star is slightly less massive and slightly smaller than the Sun ; it is also slightly cooler , but is more metal - rich.
source_text_b: WASP - 44 has a mass that is 0. 951 times that of the Sun.
explanation: Sentence 3 says WASP-44 is slightly less massive than the Sun, while sentence 21 gives a mass of 0.951 solar masses, which is still less massive but not a direct contradiction; however, sentence 3 also says it is slightly smaller and sentence 22 quantifies the radius as 0.927 solar radii, so the text is consistent overall.

source_text_a: The Indian Navy 's Advanced Technology Vessel project to design and construct a nuclear submarine took shape in the 1990s.
source_text_b: The Indian Navy 's Advanced Technology Vessel project to design and construct a nuclear submarine took shape in the 1980s.
explanation: Sentence 11 says the ATV project took shape in the 1990s, while sentence 24 says it took shape in the 1980s, so the stated decade conflicts.

source_text_a: The lead vessel of the class , INS Arihant was launched in 2009 and after extensive sea trials , was confirmed as ready for operations on 23 February 2016.
source_text_b: The first boat of the class , INS Arihant is expected to be commissioned by 2016.
explanation: Sentence 4 says INS Arihant was confirmed ready for operations on 23 February 2016, while sentence 41 says it was expected to be commissioned by 2016, which conflicts with already being operationally ready by that date in the same timeline context.

source_text_a: In Pakistan, the government lifted a six-year moratorium on the execution of civilians in the wake of the horrific Taliban attack on a school in Peshawar in December.
source_text_b: More than 60 people have been put to death since, and the government has threatened to send thousands more death row prisoners to the gallows.
explanation: Sentence 11 says Pakistan lifted a six-year moratorium, while sentence 12 says more than 60 people have already been executed since then, which conflicts only if read as describing the immediate aftermath; however they are actually compatible, so not contradictory.

source_text_a: Israel and the United States supported the Palestinians' efforts to join the body The formal accession was marked with a ceremony at The Hague, in the Netherlands, where the court is based.
source_text_b: Israel and the United States, neither of which is an ICC member, opposed the Palestinians' efforts to join the body.
explanation: Sentence 2 says Israel and the United States supported the Palestinians' efforts to join the ICC, while sentence 6 says they opposed those efforts.

source_text_a: The Palestinian Authority officially became the 123rd member of the International Criminal Court on Wednesday, a step that gives the court jurisdiction over alleged crimes in Palestinian territories.
source_text_b: "As we have said repeatedly, we do not believe that Palestine is a state and therefore we do not believe that it is eligible to join the ICC," the State Department said in a statement.
explanation: Sentence 1 states the Palestinian Authority became an ICC member, while sentence 18 says Palestine is not a state and therefore not eligible to join the ICC.

source_text_a: In the end, two agents were injured in a crash and the suspect was shot before being captured.
source_text_b: The two agents were treated at an area hospital and released, according to Emmett, who says "they are fine."
explanation: Sentence 2 says two agents were injured in a crash, while sentence 16 says the two agents were treated and released and are fine, which is inconsistent with the injury description in context.

source_text_a: In the end, two agents were injured in a crash and the suspect was shot before being captured.
source_text_b: Emmett says he's relieved that the FBI's officers and agents are OK and that the suspect is in custody.He said it was the "conclusion of a quick and easy investigation."CNN's Vivian Kuo and Ryan Scallan contributed to this report.
explanation: Sentence 2 says two agents were injured in a crash, but sentence 20 says the FBI's officers and agents are OK, creating a conflict about whether they were injured.

source_text_a: The Hope Highway was first established in 1950.
source_text_b: The Hope Highway was first established in 1928.
explanation: Sentence 3 says the highway was first established in 1950, while sentence 22 says it was first established in 1928.

source_text_a: The highway connects the city of Hope to the Seward Highway , and travels through 17 miles ( 27 km ) of the Chugach National Forest.
source_text_b: The entire length of the Hope Highway is located in the Chugach National Forest.
explanation: Sentence 4 says the highway travels through 17 miles of the Chugach National Forest, while sentence 16 says the entire length of the highway is located in that forest.

source_text_a: The highway was created circa 1928 and was designated as Forest Highway 14 by the Federal Highway Administration.
source_text_b: The Hope Highway was first established in 1928.
explanation: Sentence 6 says the highway was created circa 1928, which conflicts with sentence 22's statement that it was first established in 1928 only if treating 'created' as the same establishment date; the date itself is consistent, so this is not a true contradiction.

source_text_a: The highway connects the city of Hope to the Seward Highway , and travels through 17 miles ( 27 km ) of the Chugach National Forest.
source_text_b: The entire highway is in Kenai Peninsula Borough.
explanation: Sentence 4 implies the highway spans more than just the borough section by describing its route, while sentence 30 states the entire highway is in Kenai Peninsula Borough; these are compatible and not contradictory.

source_text_a: That's because California is a breadbasket to the nation, growing more than a third of its vegetables and nearly two-thirds of its fruits and nuts.
source_text_b: Moreover, the drop in oil prices also eases the cost of transporting food from California to the other 49 states, she said.California grows less than 10% of the nation's vegetables and fruits.
explanation: Sentence 3 says California grows more than a third of the nation's vegetables and nearly two-thirds of its fruits and nuts, while sentence 12 says it grows less than 10% of the nation's vegetables and fruits.

source_text_a: Melinda and Sylvia argue after Melinda says that the money she has inherited makes her more desirable.
source_text_b: Silvia, who is more down to earth, is infuriated by Melinda's newly haughty behaviour.
explanation: Sentence 7 says Melinda is proud/haughty about her inheritance, while sentence 8 describes Sylvia as the one behaving haughtily and being infuriated by it; the attributed person is different, so these do not contradict.

source_text_a: The sketch Jack does of his beloved is dated April 14, 1910 Their love affair was doomed like two ships passing in the night, but it was so good while it lasted for Jack and Rose in the 1997 blockbuster movie "Titanic."
source_text_b: The sketch Jack does of his beloved is dated April 14, 1912.
explanation: The two sentences give different dates for Jack’s sketch in Titanic: April 14, 1910 versus April 14, 1912.

source_text_a: However , when Mr. Krabs hears them , he warns the two never to use the word again or any of the 13 bad words.
source_text_b: However , when Mr. Krabs accidentally injures his toe , he utters all the 13 bad words.
explanation: Sentence 10 says Mr. Krabs warns them never to use the bad word again, while sentence 13 says Mr. Krabs later utters all 13 bad words, violating that prohibition.

source_text_a: Squidward then informs Mr. Krabs , who then decides to tell them that they were using profanity , and mentions that there are 13 swear words they should not use.
source_text_b: Mr. Krabs is about to give SpongeBob and Patrick the job , but he hits his foot on a rock , throws the paint away and says all 13 swear words while complaining about his foot being injured.
explanation: Sentence 23 says Mr. Krabs tells them there are 13 swear words they should not use, while sentence 31 has Mr. Krabs himself saying all 13 swear words.

source_text_a: SpongeBob and Patrick vow Mr. Krabs that they will never use swear words again.
source_text_b: Eventually , he loses the game and accidentally utters the swear word.
explanation: Sentence 24 says SpongeBob and Patrick vow never to use swear words again, but sentence 27 says SpongeBob later accidentally utters the swear word.

source_text_a: Charleston is suspected of involvement in 15 commercial robberies dating to November 2013, according to FBI officials.
source_text_b: Although he declined to give details of the 32 previous robberies, Emmett said it was an intensive investigation that was already underway as a priority for the FBI's violent crimes and major offender squad.
explanation: Sentence 4 says Charleston was suspected in 15 commercial robberies, while sentence 18 says there were 32 previous robberies, which is a direct mismatch in the reported number of robberies.

source_text_a: He describes the inevitability of death for all things at their proper time, using the destruction of an oak tree, a stone, and a river as examples, and listing all the classes of medieval society as universally subject to death.
source_text_b: He describes the possibility of immortality for all things at their proper time
explanation: Sentence 21 says Theseus describes the inevitability of death for all things, while sentence 23 says he discusses the possibility of immortality for all things, which are incompatible claims about the same topic.

source_text_a: They are the only ones who know about this short engagement, as younger sister Mary was away at school.
source_text_b: Anne visits Mary and her family, where she is well-loved.
explanation: Sentence 6 says Mary was away at school and did not know about the short engagement, while sentence 12 implies Anne visits Mary in a family setting where Mary is fully present and involved; this is compatible, so not contradictory.

source_text_a: Maurice is a Consul at Genes, a Mediterranean town where he has married Onorina the daughter of the only wealthy man into the town, although it seems he was originally extremely reluctant to get married.
source_text_b: There is also the implication that he had in fact fallen in love with Honorine himself, which is why he avoided marriage initially.
explanation: Sentence 1 says Maurice was originally extremely reluctant to get married because of his own situation, while sentence 20 says he avoided marriage initially because he had fallen in love with Honorine, giving a different reason for the same behavior.

source_text_a: This school year, she says, 145 students are Iraqi -- roughly 20 percent of the students at this state-funded institution -- with another 40 Iraqi children on a waiting list.
source_text_b: The United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees estimates up to 250,000 school-age Iraqi children are in Jordan.
explanation: Sentence 9 says the school has 145 Iraqi students, while sentence 31 estimates up to 250,000 school-age Iraqi children in Jordan, referring to a much larger count for Iraqi children in the country and thus a mismatch in quantity if taken as the same school context.

source_text_a: Jordanian Minister of Education Khalid Touqan says he expects Jordan to accommodate 40,000 to 50,000 Iraqi students this year.
source_text_b: About 10 percent of Jordan's population is now made up of Iraqi refugees -- the estimates range from 500,000 to 750,000 of them.
explanation: Sentence 15 predicts 40,000 to 50,000 Iraqi students in Jordan, whereas sentence 40 says about 10% of Jordan's population is Iraqi refugees, estimated at 500,000 to 750,000, which gives incompatible scale figures for the same population description.

source_text_a: At the age of 15 , he competed at the 2013 IPC Alpine Skiing World Cup in Mount Hutt , New Zealand , with B.J. as his guide , winning three medals.
source_text_b: The following year he competed in the 2014 Winter Paralympics in Sochi as the youngest member of the Canadian Paralympic Team at the age of 16.
explanation: Sentence 20 says Marcoux competed at age 15 in the 2013 World Cup, while sentence 23 says he competed the following year at age 16 in the 2014 Paralympics; these are different events but not contradictory.

source_text_a: Later that year he won a silver medal in the Giant Slalom at the 2013 IPC Alpine Skiing World Championships in La Molina , Spain , and became the national Slalom and Giant Slalom champion at Sun Peaks , British Columbia.
source_text_b: He won bronze in both the Downhill and the Super - G , as well as a gold in the Giant Slalom by over two seconds.
explanation: Sentence 21 says he won a silver medal in Giant Slalom at the 2013 World Championships, while sentence 25 says he won gold in Giant Slalom at the 2014 Paralympics; these are different competitions and can both be true.

source_text_a: While on the faculty at the University of Chicago , Kelsey was awarded her M.D. during 1950.
source_text_b: That same year she completed her studies and received a Ph.D. in pharmacology at the University of Chicago.
explanation: Sentence 16 says Kelsey received her Ph.D. in pharmacology in 1940, while sentence 23 says she was awarded her M.D. during 1950; both cannot be her graduate degree timeline if read as the same stage of training, but they actually refer to different degrees, so no contradiction? Wait.

source_text_a: Ann Curley uses a pump attached to a catheter in her body to monitor glucose levels and administer insulin.
source_text_b: Another life-altering development in diabetes, which I have discovered in the last year, is the continuous glucose monitor, or CGM.
explanation: Sentence 4 says Ann Curley uses a pump attached to a catheter to monitor glucose levels, while sentence 42 says the continuous glucose monitor is a separate life-altering development she discovered only in the last year, so the specific monitoring device described is inconsistent.

source_text_a: The current systems are "open looped," meaning the diabetic controls when the doses are given.
source_text_b: This means diabetics someday will be able to wear a pump that gathers data from a sensor so precise that the pump can be programmed to act on its own and make adjustments all by itself.
explanation: Sentence 50 says current systems are open looped and the diabetic controls when doses are given, whereas sentence 49 describes a future pump that acts on its own and makes adjustments by itself, which are incompatible control schemes for the same system context.

source_text_a: Hurricane Nicole was the last hurricane in the 1998 Atlantic hurricane season.
source_text_b: When Nicole lasted into December as a hurricane , it was one of only five Atlantic hurricanes on record in the month.
explanation: Sentence 2 says Nicole was the last hurricane in the 1998 Atlantic hurricane season, while sentence 37 says it was one of only five Atlantic hurricanes on record in the month, which is a different claim and not contradictory.

source_text_a: Mildred and Eduardo Gonzalez worry about what would happen to their family if she is deported.
source_text_b: His wife, Mildred, added, "We didn't come here to break the law. We just want to feel safe and have a home just like everybody else."
explanation: Sentence 3 says the couple worries about deportation, while sentence 38 explicitly says they did not come to break the law.

source_text_a: His wife faces deportation to Guatemala -- her home country that she hasn't seen since 1989.
source_text_b: In Gonzalez's case, his wife, Mildred, came to the United States with her mother in 1989 when she was 5 years old.
explanation: Sentence 4 says Mildred hasn't seen Guatemala since 1989, while sentence 21 says she came to the United States in 1989, which is incompatible with her being in Guatemala then.

source_text_a: In September 2000, Mildred's mother applied for legalization and included her daughter in that application.
source_text_b: However, six weeks earlier, Gonzalez and Mildred got married, canceling Mildred's ability to apply for legal status through her mother because she was no longer an unmarried daughter under the age of 21.
explanation: Sentence 23 says Mildred was included in her mother's 2000 legalization application, while sentence 25 says that marriage in 2000 canceled her ability to apply through her mother because she was no longer an unmarried daughter under 21, which conflicts with the earlier inclusion as an eligible dependent.

source_text_a: That's just fine, according to Mark Krikorian, the executive director of the Center for Immigration Studies, which lobbies for tougher laws on illegal immigration.
source_text_b: "What you're talking about is amnesty for illegal immigrants who have a relative in the armed forces, and that's just outrageous," he said.
explanation: Sentence 29 says Krikorian approves the situation, while sentence 30 presents it as 'amnesty' and 'outrageous,' reflecting an apparently conflicting stance on the policy.

source_text_a: Sen. John McCain, freshly back from a visit to Iraq over Thanksgiving and the most hawkish of the candidates, and Rep. Ron Paul, the most anti-war of the candidates, tangled on two occasions over the Iraq war.
source_text_b: Asked which government programs they would cut, Paul said bringing the troops home from Iraq would save "a trillion dollars."
explanation: Sentence 17 describes McCain as the most hawkish candidate, while sentence 19 presents Paul advocating bringing the troops home from Iraq, making their Iraq-war positions incompatible in the same context.

source_text_a: In a section of the debate about gun ownership rights, three of the GOP presidential hopefuls said they do not own guns: McCain, Giuliani and Romney.
source_text_b: Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson quipped, "I own a couple of guns -- but I'm not going to tell you what they are or where they are."
explanation: Sentence 32 says three candidates do not own guns, but sentence 33 says Thompson owns a couple of guns, so Thompson cannot be among the non-owners if he is one of the three in the same debate context.

source_text_a: In the wet season , air quality improves , fresh water quality improves , and vegetation grows substantially , leading to crop yields late in the season.
source_text_b: During the wet season , a combination of heavy rainfall and , in some places such as Hong Kong , an onshore wind , improve air quality.
explanation: Sentence 11 says air quality improves during the wet season generally, while sentence 42 states that in some places the combination of heavy rainfall and onshore wind improves air quality; these are compatible, not contradictory.

source_text_a: Rivers overflow their banks , and some animals retreat to higher ground.
source_text_b: Such floods cause rivers to burst their banks and submerge homes.
explanation: Sentence 12 says rivers overflow their banks in the wet season, and sentence 52 says floods cause rivers to burst their banks; the statements agree rather than conflict.

source_text_a: Ursula and Birkin become involved, and Gudrun and Gerald eventually begin a love affair.
source_text_b: Gerald and Gudrun's relationship, however, becomes stormy.
explanation: Sentence 4 says Gudrun and Gerald eventually begin a love affair, while sentence 11 says their relationship becomes stormy, which is a conflicting characterization of the same relationship state in this context.

source_text_a: Zuleika Dobson, 'though not strictly beautiful', is a devastatingly attractive young woman of the Edwardian era, a true femme fatale, who is a prestidigitator by profession, formerly a governess.
source_text_b: Zuleika's current occupation (though, more importantly, perhaps, her enrapturing beauty) has made her something of a small-time celebrity and she manages to gain entrance to the privileged, all-male domain of Oxford University because her grandfather is the Warden of Judas College (based on Merton College, Beerbohm's alma mater).
explanation: Sentence 1 says Zuleika is a prestidigitator by profession and formerly a governess, while sentence 2 centers her current occupation as the reason for her celebrity, which is a different present-role emphasis and potentially inconsistent with the prior profession statement if read as her main occupation in the same moment.

source_text_a: The Duke, instead pledging to kill himself the next day—which Zuleika more or less permits—has dinner that night with his social club where the other members also affirm their love for Zuleika.
source_text_b: Upon telling them of his plan to die, the others unexpectedly agree to also commit suicide for Zuleika.
explanation: Sentence 9 says the others affirm their love for Zuleika after the Duke mentions suicide, while sentence 10 says they unexpectedly agree to also commit suicide for Zuleika, describing the same group’s response as escalating from admiration to a shared suicide pact rather than merely affirming love.

source_text_a: Earlier, officials had said the girl, who is now 7, was 3-years-old at the time the video was made.
source_text_b: But Donohue said Tuesday, "To my understanding, the abuse occurred before she was 3 years old."
explanation: Sentence 8 says the girl was 3 years old when the video was made, while sentence 9 says the abuse occurred before she was 3 years old.

source_text_a: "Für Elise" Bob Dylan wrote "It Ain't Me, Babe" for Joan Baez.
source_text_b: Written by: Ludwig van Beethoven .
explanation: Sentence 1 attributes the work to Bob Dylan, while sentence 2 says it was written by Ludwig van Beethoven.

source_text_a: "Für Elise" Bob Dylan wrote "It Ain't Me, Babe" for Joan Baez.
source_text_b: Written for: Some girl probably not named Elise.
explanation: Sentence 1 implies the song is by Bob Dylan, but sentence 3 says it was written for 'Some girl probably not named Elise,' which conflicts with the title-based claim about 'Für Elise' only if treated as the same work's intended dedicatee; however this is not a direct contradiction, so it is not included.

source_text_a: Written by: Ludwig van Beethoven .
source_text_b: Instead, the song was originally titled "Bagatelle in A minor" based on some handwritten notation a Beethoven researcher claimed to have seen on a now-lost copy of the sheet music.
explanation: Sentence 2 identifies the piece as written by Beethoven, while sentence 5 says it was originally titled 'Bagatelle in A minor,' which is a compatible alternate title rather than a contradiction.

source_text_a: Along the way , MS 9W intersects MS 315 in Paris.
source_text_b: After crossing into Lafayette County , the route reaches the community of Paris and intersects the eastern terminus of MS 315 as well as CR 428.
explanation: Sentence 5 says MS 9W intersects MS 315 in Paris, while sentence 12 says it intersects the eastern terminus of MS 315 in Paris; these are compatible, not contradictory.

source_text_a: WASP - 44 is a G - type star in constellation Cetus without any planets in orbit.
source_text_b: = There is one known planet in the orbit of WASP - 44 : WASP - 44b.
explanation: Sentence 2 says WASP-44 has no planets in orbit, while sentence 32 says there is one known planet orbiting WASP-44.

source_text_a: Mrs. Liscom does not approve of Harriett Jameson because she is not trained in housekeeping.
source_text_b: An expected turn of events leads Mrs. Liscom to adore Harriett though, and the two get married at the end of the novel.
explanation: Sentence 28 says Mrs. Liscom does not approve of Harriett Jameson, while sentence 29 says she comes to adore Harriett, which directly flips her attitude toward the same person.

source_text_a: An expected turn of events leads Mrs. Liscom to adore Harriett though, and the two get married at the end of the novel.
source_text_b: Harriett does not suffer from any severe wounds, and takes housekeeping lessons from Mrs. Liscom until the wedding.
explanation: Sentence 29 says Harriett and Liscom get married at the end of the novel, while sentence 35 says Harriett takes housekeeping lessons from Mrs. Liscom until the wedding, implying the wedding has not yet occurred at that point.

source_text_a: She spent most of her career in the Mediterranean , although she was sent to China during the Boxer Rebellion of 1900 – 01.
source_text_b: She supported Allied forces during the later stages of the Boxer Rebellion in mid - 1901 before returning to Toulon on 8 November.
explanation: Sentence 3 says Amiral Charner was sent to China during the Boxer Rebellion, while sentence 43 says she was sent to the Far East/Indochina and supported Allied forces later in the Boxer Rebellion; these accounts conflict on the ship’s Boxer Rebellion deployment location and timing.

source_text_a: The ship was assigned to the International Squadron off the island of Crete during the Greco - Turkish War of 1897 to protect French interests and citizens.
source_text_b: The ship was sent to Crete on 10 February 1897 as part of the French contingent of the International Squadron deployed there during the Greco - Turkish War to protect Western interests and citizens and remained with the squadron until November 1898.
explanation: Sentence 4 says the ship served off Crete during the Greco-Turkish War to protect French interests, while sentence 37 says she was sent to Crete during that war to protect Western interests; the protected interests are described differently for the same mission.

source_text_a: During this time , she helped to rescue several thousand Armenians from Syria during the Armenian Genocide of 1915.
source_text_b: On 11 – 12 September , the ship participated in the rescue of 3 , 000 Armenians north of the Orontes River Delta from pursuing Ottoman troops.
explanation: Sentence 7 says she helped rescue several thousand Armenians, while sentence 52 specifies 3,000 Armenians; these are incompatible quantities for the same rescue event.

source_text_a: Amiral Charner was sunk in early 1916 by a German submarine , with only a single survivor rescued.
source_text_b: Some 427 men were lost , with only a single survivor rescued five days later.
explanation: Sentence 8 says the ship was sunk in early 1916 with only a single survivor rescued, and sentence 56 says only a single survivor was rescued five days later after the sinking; the survival detail is compatible but the rescue timing and phrasing are not directly contradictory, so this is not included.

source_text_a: Amiral Charner was sunk in early 1916 by a German submarine , with only a single survivor rescued.
source_text_b: She sank in only two minutes with the loss of nearly the entire crew.
explanation: Sentence 8 says the ship was sunk by a German submarine and only a single survivor was rescued, while sentence 55 says she sank in two minutes with the loss of nearly the entire crew; this is compatible overall and not a true contradiction, so it is excluded.

source_text_a: H - 13 is a county - designated highway in the Upper Peninsula of the US state of Michigan that runs north – south for approximately 36. 2 miles ( 58. 3 km ) between Nahma Junction in Delta County and the Munising area in Alger County.
source_text_b: The H - 13 designation debuted in 1972 between Nahma Junction and H - 58 ; H - 13 turned east and ran concurrently with H - 58 before turning north on Miners Castle Road.
explanation: Sentence 2 says H-13 runs between Nahma Junction and the Munising area, while sentence 34 says the designation debuted between Nahma Junction and H-58 before turning north on Miners Castle Road.

source_text_a: A section of H - 13 in Alger County originally continued north to Miners Castle , but the roadway designation was truncated in the first decade of the 21st century.
source_text_b: The H - 58 concurrency was removed in 2004 when the northern segment of H - 13 along Miners Castle Road was redesignated H - 11.
explanation: Sentence 8 says the northward continuation to Miners Castle was truncated in the first decade of the 21st century, while sentence 36 says that northern segment was redesignated H-11 in 2004 rather than simply truncated.

source_text_a: At that junction , the FFH - 13 designation ends and H - 13 continues north as a gravel surface along Connors Road through Munising Township.
source_text_b: The H - 58 concurrency was removed in 2004 when the northern segment of H - 13 along Miners Castle Road was redesignated H - 11.
explanation: Sentence 22 says the FFH-13 designation ends at M-28/M-94 and H-13 continues north on Connors Road, while sentence 36 says the northern segment along Miners Castle Road was redesignated H-11, indicating a different continuation for the northern segment.

source_text_a: The 138th Boat Race took place on 4 April 1992.
source_text_b: First held in 1829 , the race takes place on the 4. 2 - mile ( 6. 8 km ) Championship Course on the River Thames in southwest London.
explanation: Sentence 2 says the 138th Boat Race took place in 1992, while sentence 11 says the first Boat Race was in 1829; these are not contradictory because they refer to different races and time points.

source_text_a: WASHINGTON (CNN)  -- The confirmation of Michael Mukasey as attorney general was all but assured Friday when two key Democratic senators said they will vote in favor of the nominee despite questions about his views on "waterboarding" and the president's power to order electronic surveillance.
source_text_b: Sens. Dianne Feinstein of California and Charles Schumer of New York announced they would support the retired federal judge from New York just hours after the chairman of the Judiciary Committee announced his opposition to the nominee.
explanation: Sentence 1 says two key Democratic senators will vote in favor of Mukasey, while sentence 2 names those senators and says the same thing, so these are consistent, not contradictory.

source_text_a: While saying "serious questions have been raised about Judge Mukasey's views on torture and on separation of powers," Feinstein said she would support the nominee because the Justice Department needed fresh leadership.
source_text_b: Sens. Dianne Feinstein of California and Charles Schumer of New York announced they would support the retired federal judge from New York just hours after the chairman of the Judiciary Committee announced his opposition to the nominee.
explanation: Sentence 15 says Feinstein will support the nominee, and sentence 2 says Feinstein and Schumer will support him; these agree rather than conflict.

source_text_a: Chanzy was an Amiral Charner - class armored cruiser built for the French Navy in the 1890s.
source_text_b: She was launched on 24 January 1894 and initially was commissioned for her sea trials on 6 February.
explanation: Sentence 2 says Chanzy was built in the 1890s, but sentence 28 says she was laid down in 1894 after being ordered in 1899, which is an impossible construction chronology clash for the same ship.

source_text_a: Upon completion , she served in the Mediterranean Squadron and she was assigned to the International Squadron off the island of Crete during the Greco - Turkish War of 1897 to protect French interests and citizens.
source_text_b: The ship began trials on 28 December before she was sent to Crete on 16 February 1897 as part of the French contingent of the International Squadron deployed there during the Greco - Turkish War to protect Western interests and citizens.
explanation: Sentence 3 says Chanzy was assigned to the International Squadron off Crete during the Greco-Turkish War, while sentence 33 says she was sent to Crete as part of the French contingent of the International Squadron; these differ in wording about the same deployment and are not truly incompatible.

source_text_a: A former girlfriend of Stiles' said that, before the arrest, she lived in fear after going to police to identify the suspect after seeing enhanced photos from the videotape on the local news.
source_text_b: Another former girlfriend of Stiles', Tina Allen, said this month she thinks she is the reason Stiles came in contact with the girl and is "mortified" by the allegations against him.
explanation: Sentence 19 says Elaine Thomas went to police before the arrest after identifying the suspect, while sentence 24 says Tina Allen thinks she is the reason Stiles came in contact with the girl, which are different account claims and do not directly contradict; no contradiction actually exists.

source_text_a: He joins the cavalry regiment of the Duc de Maufrigneuse and the Vicomte de Sérizy, son of the Comte de Sérizy - the same young nobleman Oscar claimed to be acquainted with in the coach on the road to L'Isle-Adam.
source_text_b: Although the vicomte dies shortly afterwards, the Comte de Sérizy is grateful and forgives Oscar for his earlier indiscretion.
explanation: Sentence 21 says the Vicomte de Sérizy is the son of the Comte de Sérizy, while sentence 25 says the vicomte dies shortly afterwards; this creates no direct contradiction by itself, but sentence 24 identifies the same vicomte as being rescued and sentence 25 says he later dies, so these are compatible. No contradiction.

source_text_a: He is put to work under Mr Rossiter in the Postage Department, replacing a youth named Bannister, and is befriended by Mr Waller, a kindly employee of the bank, who takes him to lunch; on his return, he is joined by Psmith, also a new employee, in the same department as Mike.
source_text_b: After a while, a new man starts, and Mike is moved on to the Cash Department, under Mr Waller.
explanation: Sentence 7 says Mike is in the Postage Department under Mr Rossiter, while sentence 17 says he is moved to the Cash Department under Mr Waller, so they assign him to different departments/supervisors at the same point in the narrative.

source_text_a: To save the man's job, Mike takes the blame, and is fired and roasted by Bickersdyke.
source_text_b: Bickersdyke, furious, agrees to keep Mike on at the bank.
explanation: Sentence 22 says Mike is fired after taking the blame, while sentence 24 says Bickersdyke agrees to keep Mike on at the bank, which directly conflicts with being dismissed.

source_text_a: Lord Hartwicke ruled that abridgements fell under two categories : " true abridgements " and " coloured shortenings ".
source_text_b: However , Lord Hardwicke drew a distinction between works " fairly made " and those " colourably shortened ".
explanation: Sentence 7 says the judge ruled abridgements fell into two categories, while sentence 34 describes the distinction as between works 'fairly made' and those 'colourably shortened'; these are different labels for the same legal classification and are presented inconsistently in the document.

source_text_a: Philip Yorke , 1st Earl of Hardwicke presided over and decided the case.
source_text_b: The case was heard and the opinion written by Philip Yorke , 1st Earl of Hardwicke , and concerned Fletcher Gyles , a bookseller who had published a copy of Matthew Hale 's Pleas of the Crown.
explanation: Sentence 20 says Philip Yorke presided over and decided the case, while sentence 3 says he heard the case and wrote the opinion; these are compatible, so this is not a true contradiction.
